verses.text: Zedekiah began to reign at twenty-one and did evil; through the LORD's anger Judah was cast out of His presence. The cause is named. A nation's ruin springs from the sins of leaders and people alike.
Zedekiah began to reign at twenty-one and did evil; through the LORD's anger Judah was cast out of His presence. The cause is named. A nation's ruin springs from the sins of leaders and people alike.
Zedekiah began to reign at twenty-one and did evil; through the LORD's anger Judah was cast out of His presence. The cause is named. A nation's ruin springs from the sins of leaders and people alike.

Babylon besieges the city; famine grows fierce; at last the wall is breached and the men of war flee by night. The end comes. Long-delayed judgment falls at the appointed hour, and flight is too late.

Zedekiah is captured; his sons are slain before his eyes, then his eyes are put out, and he dies a prisoner in Babylon. A bitter fulfilment. Every word God spoke against him came exactly to pass.

The guard burns the temple, the palace, and every great house, breaks down the walls, and carries the people away, leaving only the poor. The city is undone. What centuries had built, sin brought to ashes.

They break up the pillars, the brasen sea, and the sacred vessels, carrying off the brass, silver, and gold. The holy things are plundered. Outward splendour cannot stand where the heart has forsaken God.

They break up the pillars, the brasen sea, and the sacred vessels, carrying off the brass, silver, and gold. The holy things are plundered. Outward splendour cannot stand where the heart has forsaken God.

The two great pillars, the sea, and the twelve oxen are recorded, with the pomegranates round the capitals. The glory is catalogued as it departs. Scripture mourns the loss of what once adorned God's house.

The chief priest, officers, and sixty men of the people are taken to Riblah and slain; 'thus Judah was carried away captive out of his own land.' The sentence is complete. The long warned-of exile is now history.

The exact numbers carried away in Nebuchadnezzar's several campaigns are set down. God keeps a precise account. Not one of His people is lost or forgotten in the reckoning.

In the thirty-seventh year of exile, Evil-merodach lifts up Jehoiachin, sets his throne above the other kings, and gives him a daily portion for life. The book ends in hope. Even in captivity, God's mercy raises the humbled and keeps David's line alive.
